subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the inductive reactance is

XL=ωL   ...1

The formula to calculate capacitive reactance is

XC=1ωC   ...2

As can be seen from equation (1), the inductive reactance varies directly with the frequency and equation (2) indicates that capacitive reactance varies inversely with the frequency.

Hence, curve A represents the capacitive reactance and curve B represents the inductive reactance.

solution:
Since the voltage across the inductor and capacitor is same, the circuit is in resonance. Therefore, the current in the circuit will be,

i=VR=2205=44 A.

The voltage across the inductor is double that across the resistor. Therefore, VL=2VR=440 V.

We know that VL=iXL=i×2πfL⇒L=44044×100π=110π H=100πmH

Therefore, K=1100=0.01≈0.

For an LCR circuit. the resonant angular frequency is given by, ω0=1LC=104 rad s-1

The given frequency is 60% lower than resonant frequency. Therefore,

ω'=0.4×104=4000 rad s-1

Reactance of the capacitor at given frequency,

XC=ω'C-1=250 Ω.

Reactance of the inductor at given frequency,

XL=ω'L=40 Ω

Now the amplitude of the current in the given circuit will be,

i0=V0R2+XC'-XL'2=50102+250-402=238 mA

solution:
In resonance $Z=R$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{R} \rightarrow \text { halved } \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{I}\end{aligned}$
I becomes doubled.

subject: Physics
solution:
Net reactance: $X = X_C - X_L = 150 - 70 = 80Ω$.
Impedance: $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 80^2} = \sqrt{10000} = 100Ω$.
Power factor: $\cos\phi = R/Z = 60/100 = 0.6$.
Since power factor is $\alpha/10$, we have $0.6 = \alpha/10$, so $\alpha = 6$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given $L = 1.6\text{ H}$ and $C = 40\ \mu\text{F} = 40 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F}$.

At resonance, the angular frequency is $\omega_r = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.

The inductive reactance at resonance is $X_L = \omega_r L = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{LC}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.

Substituting the given values:

$X_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.6}{40 \times 10^{-6}}}$

$X_L = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.6}{4 \times 10^{-5}}}$

$X_L = \sqrt{4 \times 10^4}$

$X_L = 200\ \Omega$

Answer: $200$

subject: Physics
solution:
In case of series RLC circuit, Equation of voltage is given by
$$
\mathrm{V}^2=V_R^2+\left(V_L-V_C^2\right)
$$
Here, $V=220 V ; V_L=V_C=300 \mathrm{~V}$
$$
\therefore V_R=\sqrt{V^2}=220 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Current $i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{100}=2.2 \mathrm{~A}$

solution:
Impedance $(Z)$ of the series LCR circuit is
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}$
At resonance, $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}$
Therefore, $Z_{\text {minimum }}=R$

subject: Physics
solution:
In an LC circuit, energy oscillates sinusoidally.
Initial energy in capacitor is $E_0 = \frac{1}{2}CV_0^2$.
When 25% is transferred to the inductor, 75% remains in the capacitor.
Since energy in capacitor varies as $E_C(t) = E_0\cos^2(\omega t)$, we need $\cos^2(\omega t) = 0.75$.
This gives $\sin^2(\omega t) = 0.25$, so $\sin(\omega t) = 0.5$, yielding $\omega t = \frac{\pi}{6}$.
With $\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$, we get $t = \frac{\pi\sqrt{LC}}{6}$ seconds.

subject: Physics
solution:
Power factor ${ }_{\text {(old) }}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}{ }^2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+(2 \mathrm{R})^2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{5} \mathrm{R}}$
Power factor ${ }_{\text {(new) }}$
$=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}}=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+(2 R-R)^2}}$
$=\frac{R}{\sqrt{2} R}$
$\therefore \frac{\text { New power factor }}{\text { Old power factor }}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{2} \mathrm{R}}}{\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\sqrt{5} \mathrm{R}}}=\sqrt{\frac{5}{2}}$

Where L,R,C are the inductance, resistance, and conductance of the L.C.R. circuit. The given resonance frequency is, ω0=1LC

⇒C=1ω0L  ...(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

⇒Q=1RL×ω0L⇒Q=ω0LR

solution:
The formula for impedance is given by 

Z=R2+XL-XC2

At resonance, XL=XC. So, Zmin=R

Power will be maximum when impedance is minimum. Thus the maximum power occurs in a purely resistive circuit.

Statement-II $\mathrm{I}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)$ in purely resistive circuit.

solution:
When the capacitor is fully discharged, the current is maximum and all the energy resides in the inductor. Hence, the energy of the capacitor is equal to the energy of the inductor.

Li2m2=Q2m2C   ...(i)

 where im is the maximum current and Qm is the maximum charge.

The given values are 

Qm=2.7×10-6 CL=75×10-3 HC=1.2×10-6 F

The maximum current is found from equation (i)

im=1LCQm⇒im=2.7×10-675×10-3×1.2×10-6=9 mA
